text:

Sentence 2: Point out limitations or gaps in prior studies.

Sentence 3: Introduce the specific research question or hypothesis.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Purpose
Detail the procedures/materials used to interpret the data and ensure reproducibility.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Scope
Approximate 800-1200 words, 4-6 paragraphs.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Structure

Paragraph 1: Study Design Overview

Sentence 1: Describe the study type (e.g., experimental, observational).

Sentence 2: Specify the study setting or environment.

Sentence 3: Outline the timeline or duration.



Paragraph 2: Participants / Samples

Sentence 1: Describe the selection criteria.

Sentence 2: Provide demographic or characteristic information about study population.



Paragraph 3: Data Collection Methods

Sentence 1: Detail instruments or tools used.

Sentence 2: Explain procedures or protocols followed.

Sentence 3: Note any calibrations or standardizations.



Paragraph 4: Data Analysis Techniques

Sentence 1: Specify statistical tests or models applied.

Sentence 2: Define significance levels or confidence intervals.

Sentence 3: Mention software or tools used for analysis.



Paragraph 5: Ethical Approvals
Include information about ethical approvals and permits (or at end of paper).

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Purpose
Present the findings (tables, graphics) without interpretation.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Scope
Approximate 600-1000 words, 3-5 paragraphs.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Structure

Paragraph 1: Primary Outcomes

Sentence 1: State the main result.

Sentence 2: Provide supporting data or statistics.

Sentence 3: Refer to relevant tables or figures.



Paragraph 2: Secondary Outcomes

Sentence 1: Describe additional significant findings.

Sentence 2: Offer corresponding data or metrics.

Sentence 3: Reference supplementary materials if applicable.



Paragraph 3: Exploratory / Unexpected Results

Sentence 1: Highlight unanticipated findings.

Sentence 2: Provide relevant data.

Sentence 3: Suggest potential implications or need for further investigation.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Purpose
Interpret the results, compare with existing literature, discuss implications, suggest future directions for research.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Scope
Approximately 800-1000 words, 4-6 paragraphs.

title: Research Paper Outline
text:
Structure

Paragraph 1: Summary of Key Findings

Sentence 1: Restate the study’s main finding(s) concisely.

Sentence 2: Emphasize the significance of these findings.

Sentence 3: Connect findings to the research question.



Paragraph 2: Comparison with Previous Work

Sentence 1: Relate results to prior studies.

Sentence 2: Highlight agreements with existing literature.

Sentence 3: Point out contradictions or novel contributions.



Paragraph 3: Mechanisms & Interpretation

Sentence 1: Explain why these results occurred.

Sentence 2: Suggest possible biological, chemical, or physical mechanisms.

Sentence 3: Consider alternative explanations (be objective and self-critical).



Paragraph 4: Limitations of the Study

Sentence 1: Acknowledge potential weaknesses (e.g., sample size, methods).

Sentence 2: Discuss how limitations might affect interpretation.

Sentence 3: Suggest how future studies could address these issues.



Paragraph 5: Broader Implications

Sentence 1: Explain how the findings impact the field.

Sentence 2: Discuss potential applications in science, policy, or beyond.

Sentence 3: Connect to real-world challenges or future advancements.



Paragraph 6: Future Research Directions

Sentence 1: Identify open questions or unresolved issues.

Sentence 2: Propose follow-up experiments or studies.

Sentence 3: End with a strong take-home message.

title: Research
text:
Main project areas
At the Behavioral Complexity Lab, we investigate how animals sense, communicate, and make decisions within complex ecological and social systems, focusing on tropical ecosystems like Panama and Hawaii. Combining field-based natural history, bioacoustics, custom sensors, experiments, and large-scale monitoring with information theory, AI, structural causal modeling (SCM), and network analysis, we map behavioral networks, track information flow, and identify the causal processes through which communication, competition, predation, and environmental change shape populations and communities. Our main project areas include:



Project PROBE (Practices, Reproducibility, Objectivity, and Bias in Ecology)
PROBE examines how analytical choices influence scientific conclusions and contribute to bias. Using causal diagrams (DAGs) and SCM-based workflows, we evaluate how different modeling decisions (kitchen-sink modeling and classical Structural Equation Modeling) target different quantities. We also seek to understand how how methods spread and how they broaden or canalize research ideas. We also study how SCMs and estimand-aware workflow can be effectively integrated into ecological research by identifying how researchers can adopt causal logic more broadly to strengthen ecological inference.





Tracking bias-inducing and bias-reducing practices in ecology
Defining the estimand-aware workflow
Decisions during data analysis: working to reduce bias
Statistical epidemics in biology




Animal Communication & Contest Dynamics: Our research investigates how animals use signals to navigate competitive and social interactions. Using acoustic monitoring, deep learning, experiments, and network analysis, we study how individuals assess rivals, coordinate behavior, and decide when to escalate or retreat. Framed within causal models of signaling and appraisal, we study how communication mediates territorial dynamics and shapes decision-making and assessment strategies.





Experimental tests of the function of vocal duetting in tropical birds
Context-dependent information transfer in birds
Exploring within-species variation in suboscine song
Quantification of signal display complexity in birds
Joint resource holding potential and and contest assessment strategies in tropical birds
Detection of neighbor-stranger recognition using complex interaction networks
Individual vocal discrimination in non-Oscine birds
Memory in territorial birds
Assessment of numerical advantage in animal contests
Vocal correlates of fighting ability in tropical birds




Hawaii VINE Project (Vertebrate Introductions in Novel Ecosystems): The multi-institutional, multi-PI VINE Project (started in 2014) investigates how introduced vertebrates integrate into and reshape ecological networks. Using causal network models, my lab studies how interactions shift across environments, how disturbance alters connectivity, and how biodiversity supports resilience and community reorganization.





Causal inference in conservation ecology
Importance of abiotic and biotic factors on network structure in novel ecosystems
Spatial variation in complex ecological networks
Large-scale experimental tests of network resilience in a novel ecosystem in Hawaii




Panama PLUMAS (Precipitation and Land Use Effects on Multiple Avian Species): PLUMAS examines how rainfall and land-use change affect bird behavior, populations, and communities. We use SCM-guided analyses to separate climate-driven effects from behavioral and ecological feedbacks, linking individual decisions to large-scale environmental change.





Fragmentation and precipitation as causal drivers of morphology, physiology, behavior, and community structuring in tropical birds
Eavesdropping in army-ant-following birds
Olfactory cues for detecting army-ant swarms in army any following birds
Chronic physiological stress along precipitation and fragmentation gradients
Bite force in insectivorous birds
Causal drivers of three-dimensional territorial size




Project BITE (Biotic Interactions in Threat Ecology)
Project BITE investigates how predation risk shapes behavior, sensory systems, and ecological interactions. Working largely in the tropics, we study how animals detect predators, protect offspring, and evolve survival-enhancing traits, revealing how predators structure communities.





Fluctuating natural selection on nest survival of tropical birds
Visual & auditory predation risk cues (army ants)

BCI effect: extreme predation risk

The shape of natural selection of nest and traits
Visual assessment of risk in jumping spiders
Multidimensional acoustic interaction networks in tropical forests




Deep Tropics Project
The Deep Tropics Project studies biodiversity, communication, and species interactions in tropical ecosystems. Integrating acoustic monitoring, AI, and causal network modeling, we examine how environmental change and disturbance propagate through behavioral systems to shape community structure and ecosystem responses.





Modeling behavioral response networks as large language models
Army ant swarm olfactory cues

Soundscapes across tropical gradients

SonoBEAST modular acoustic sensor platform

title: About Patrick
text:
About Me (Patrick Kelley)
As a trained scientist with a Ph.D. in Animal Behavior, I am interested in any sort of complex animal behavior. The more difficult and complex an organism or a group of organisms are, the more interested I become. My interests and work have spanned multiple fields within behavioral ecology, including bioacoustics, plant-animal interactions, endocrinology, visual perception, telemetry, climatic modeling, movement ecology, and ecological simulations. I have conducted fieldwork across the U.S. and in Central and South America, including Panama, Brazil, and Ecuador. My main work has focused on the tropical forests of Panama, where I have studied the ecology of tropical birds since 2000, using advanced statistical methods to better understand information flow and ecological interactions.










Education and Appointments (click to expand)

Education:

University of California-Davis (Ph.D. Animal Behavior; advisor: John C. Wingfield)
Harvard (B.A. Biology)

Postdoctoral Research Appointments:

University of California, Berkeley
Florida State University
University of British Columbia

Appointments:

[2018 - present] Research Scientist (Fixed Term; full faculty and PI status), Univ. of Wyoming
[2025 - 2026] Faculty Fellow, School of Computing, Univ. of Wyoming
[2023 - present] Adjunct Faculty, School of Computing, University of Wyoming
[2022 - present] Research Associate, Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute
[2013 - present] Research Associate, Center for Tropical Studies (UCLA)






Personal miscellany (click to reveal too much information)

Dad twice over to two uncaged yahoos, an always-improving husband, fly-fisherman (freshwater and saltwater), fiercely anti-BS, ever-reverent Eagle Scout, college rower (6-seat or stroke, port-side), lover of simple and functional outdoor gear, data nerd, a pretty good shot with a 12-gauge and a .22 rifle (and, once upon a time, a .38), very amateur artist, collector of Silver/Bronze Age comic books, lover of colorful socks, tinkerer, son of a sin-eater Vietnam vet, Scotland-born, raised in coastal Georgia in a town smaller than Laramie. I’ve been sucker-punched by a Harpy Eagle; I’ve been an accommodating host to several tropical parasites, including one that ate a hole in my arm; Dengue and a couple of unknown diseases almost killed me; I’ve had my nose broken by a large branch that fell from the rainforest canopy (poor aim, some say); and I’m the only person I’ve ever known to have fallen into a human sewage cistern, which had been stewing for weeks in the tropical heat.





Traditional academic bean-counting (click to expand)


Google Scholar Profile
Research Gate




Teaching
I currently teach three courses per year at University of Wyoming (two during Fall term and one in January term):

Behavioral Ecology (ZOO-4415) or Introductory Biology (LIFE-1010) [alternating Fall terms]
Quantitative Analysis of Messy Data (ZOO-5500) [once per year]
Data Science Deep Dive or Calling BS in Our Data-driven World (Honors) [alternative January terms]

Past courses include:

Panama Field Course (WyoPanama): an ecology-based research course in January (led three times). Link to WyoPanama 1.0 and 2.0 and WyoPanama 3.0. Link to Facebook.
Population Ecology (ZOO4400)

Other information: I am trained in Universal Design in Learning (UDL) best practices and am a strong proponent of Specifications Based Grading (links: 1, 2, 3) .



M.S./Ph.D. committees: Interested in me serving on your committee?


I have served or chaired several committees, and I generally really love the amazing interactions. I am very happy to serve on anyone’s thesis/dissertation committee, especially for those students interested in causal inference, behavioral ecology, and quantitative approaches to complex field data. I am committed to working with your advisor to help you make the progress you want. However, you should be aware that my approach to ecological inference is grounded in structural causal modeling (SCM) and a very structured estimand-first workflow.

What does this mean? Ecological research often centers on identifying and filling gaps in collective knowledge. My perspective, which is backed up by many formal scientific critiques over the last 10 years, is that many of these gaps arise not from a lack of data about specific systems, but from analyses that are not tied to clearly defined causal questions. That is, when the inferential target is ambiguous or ill-defined (as often happens in AIC multi-model selection), seemingly objective modeling choices destroy ecologists’ causal assumptions and silently answer very different and very unanticipated questions, making it difficult to determine what has actually been learned.





This perspective often differs from more traditional approaches in ecology, including “kitchen-sink” modeling strategies (e.g., including many covariates without a clear causal target, conditioning on mediators, or using model selection to infer variable importance). At the same time, nearly all ecological studies –including most of those using these approaches– use causal verbiage to interpret results, even when analyses are not aligned with explicit causal questions. My perspectives are therefore often at odds with purportedly “causal” frameworks like Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) which are great but unfortunately often applied in ways that break causal assumptions and obscure original questions. This is all to say that my transparent and literature-backed feedback to you may challenge common analytical practices. At times, my perspective may differ greatly from the guidance provided by your advisor(s) or other committee members. And that is alright! We should have differing perspectives based on our expertise with field work, different taxa, etc. When serving on a committee, you can expect me to:

ask for a very clearly defined causal question (what effect you are trying to estimate)
ask for formal literature and/or data-driven representation of your causal assumptions (e.g., a Directed Acyclic Graph, or DAG)
emphasize transparent alignment between your causal question and your statistical model(s)
be very candid about potential biases introduced by model specification

This is not about enforcing a single “correct” way to analyze your data (e.g. epistemic gate-keeping). It is about ensuring that your ecological inference is logically associated with the specific questions that you asking.
If you are considering asking me to serve on your committee, you should be very open to:

revisiting the framing of your original research questions (with respect to what effects are identifiable)
thinking carefully about all causal assumptions (even ones that may influence your system but were not measured)

engaging in difficult discussions that may push against standard practices in the field of ecology

If my perspective and these approaches sound useful (or at least interesting), I’m very happy to be involved as a member of your thesis/dissertation committee!

title: BCL
text:
What we do
The Behavioral Complexity Lab studies how animals sense, communicate, and make decisions by focusing on the causal processes that generate ecological and social patterns in the wild. We pair intensive fieldwork with transparent, reproducible workflows to track how information moves through ecosystems and link environmental constraints, social interactions, organismal traits, and observation processes to behavior, biodiversity, and community organization. This work is organized around a set of approaches that are designed to push ecological research toward clearer, mechanism-based inference (rather than muddled ecological inference):

Structural causal modeling (SCMs) and DAG-based workflows that focus on explicit, estimand-driven questions
Novel field-deployable sensor systems that expand what can be measured in natural systems and bring behavior and environment into the same causal framework
Information-centric approaches that treat signals, cues, and soundscapes as causal drivers of behavior and ecological interactions
AI-assisted and computational methods that scale the extraction and synthesis of complex behavioral and acoustic data for causal analysis
Estimand-driven workflows and re-analysis of ecological studies that move the field away from undirected “kitchen-sink” models and clarify how causal assumptions shape ecological theory







TipNews from the lab





[04/2026] Lots of big development for Zach Holmes (PhD student) this month! First, Zach successfully defended his PhD proposal. He gave a wonderful talk about an ambitious project and then defended his proposal (fielding questions about proposed analysis that I’ve never seen asked of other students)! Zach also received a $4,000 Biodiversity Graduate Student Research Enhancement Grant to study the role of acoustic information on bird-plant interactions in Hawaii! Congratulations! And then, he tells me that three papers that were co-authored were recently published; these papers are ones he worked on during his MS degree at University of Florida:

Use of Nest Boxes by Eastern Bluebirds in Rural South-Central Florida
Nocturnal at Noon? Addressing False Positives in Activity Pattern Data
How wildlife respond to tropical cyclones: short-term tactics and long-term impacts


[03/2026] Michael Castaño (MS student) successfully defends in Master’s Thesis and will be graduating this semester! Way to go, Michael! Congratulations!!!!

[01/2026] Zach Holmes (PhD student) receives word from Titley Scientific that they will support his work deploying recording units at his field site in Hawaii. Congratulations! Michael Castaño (MS student) begins working on novel applications of AI in tropical ecosystems (funded by UW School of Computing)

[09-12/2025] PI Patrick Kelley is on sabbatical studying Structural Causal Modeling.

title: Writing a short science paper (for those with short attention spans)
text: This is test!

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
In behavioral ecology, understanding interactions between species requires more than just statistical analysis. Passive acoustic recordings, camera traps, or observational data provide rich datasets—but they are ften noisy, incomplete, and influenced by complex ecological factors. The NESTED framework offers a logical, repeatable pipeline for tackling questions that integrate statistical modeling with ecological validation, always grounded in natural history knowledge. This is not anything novel; it just helps to have a framework for keeping our thoughts in order!






Note1. Name the Alternative Hypotheses





The first step is to explicitly define the competing hypotheses that might explain the patterns we observe:

What are the plausible ways one species could affect another?

Could observed patterns result from confounding factors such as habitat, season, or detectability?

Which hypotheses are consistent with known natural-history traits (activity patterns, response times, social behavior)?

By naming the alternatives upfront, we create a clear roadmap for testing and ensure that subsequent modeling is hypothesis-driven and ecologically informed.









Note2. Embed Natural History





Next, we explicitly embed the the natural history and ecology of the study species (or group of species) into the study design and data processing:

Select temporal and spatial scales that match behavior (e.g., 10-second bins for vocal interactions). This is where one could disgree with Levin 1992.

Select auxiliary data (temperature, light, noise, location) that could be relevant to your study questions.

Plan manipulative or observational experiments that are ethical and feasible, accounting for the natural history and ecology of the species.

Together, these guarantee that the dataset captures the variation that actually matters in nature.









Note3. Structured Data Collection and Processing





I transform raw observations into features suitable for analysis:

For acoustic data, detect and classify calls to produce timestamped event tables.

Aggregate into time bins, calculating call rates, durations, spectral properties, or other biologically relevant measures.

Create time-lagged features to capture potential delayed responses between species.

A well-structured dataset is essential for reproducible and interpretable results.









Note4. Test with Models





Statistical models quantify associations and potentially causal relationships while respecting ecological realism:

Begin with exploratory analysis (descriptive stats, cross-correlation) to understand your data.

Use hierarchical or time-series models (GLMMs, Bayesian state-space models) to account for variation among sites, recorders, or individuals.

Incorporate observation error where detection is imperfect.

Incorporate causal structures and modeling (Bayesian Dynamic Causal Models) into your workflow.

Examine biologically plausible lagged effects to assess temporal dynamics.

This step separates ecological signal from observation noise.









Note5. Ecological Validation





Predictions are only meaningful if validated against nature:

Use independent field observations or ethical experiments to check model outputs.

Compare patterns with known natural history to ensure plausibility.

Perform internal validation through cross-validation, simulation, and residual diagnostics.

Validation ensures that statistical associations reflect true ecological processes.









Note6. Deliver & Iterate





Finally, I communicate and refine findings to produce a deliverable (and then reiterate!):

Interpret model results in ecological terms (e.g., probability change in bird song following a monkey call).

Report uncertainty with credible or prediction intervals.

Iterate the model and study design based on validation feedback, refining features, lag windows, or model structure.

This iterative approach produces robust, biologically meaningful insights that can guide conservation, management, or further experimentation.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
In behavioral ecology, understanding interactions between species requires more than just statistical analysis. Passive acoustic recordings, camera traps, or observational data provide rich datasets—but they are ften noisy, incomplete, and influenced by complex ecological factors. The NESTED framework offers a logical, repeatable pipeline for tackling questions that integrate statistical modeling with ecological validation, always grounded in natural history knowledge. This is not anything novel; it just helps to have a framework for keeping our thoughts in order!






Note1. Name the Alternative Hypotheses





The first step is to explicitly define the competing hypotheses that might explain the patterns we observe:

What are the plausible ways one species could affect another?

Could observed patterns result from confounding factors such as habitat, season, or detectability?

Which hypotheses are consistent with known natural-history traits (activity patterns, response times, social behavior)?

By naming the alternatives upfront, we create a clear roadmap for testing and ensure that subsequent modeling is hypothesis-driven and ecologically informed.









Note2. Embed Natural History





Next, we explicitly embed the the natural history and ecology of the study species (or group of species) into the study design and data processing:

Select temporal and spatial scales that match behavior (e.g., 10-second bins for vocal interactions). This is where one could disgree with Levin 1992.

Select auxiliary data (temperature, light, noise, location) that could be relevant to your study questions.

Plan manipulative or observational experiments that are ethical and feasible, accounting for the natural history and ecology of the species.

Together, these guarantee that the dataset captures the variation that actually matters in nature.









Note3. Structured Data Collection and Processing





I transform raw observations into features suitable for analysis:

For acoustic data, detect and classify calls to produce timestamped event tables.

Aggregate into time bins, calculating call rates, durations, spectral properties, or other biologically relevant measures.

Create time-lagged features to capture potential delayed responses between species.

A well-structured dataset is essential for reproducible and interpretable results.









Note4. Test with Models





Statistical models quantify associations and potentially causal relationships while respecting ecological realism:

Begin with exploratory analysis (descriptive stats, cross-correlation) to understand your data.

Use hierarchical or time-series models (GLMMs, Bayesian state-space models) to account for variation among sites, recorders, or individuals.

Incorporate observation error where detection is imperfect.

Incorporate causal structures and modeling (Bayesian Dynamic Causal Models) into your workflow.

Examine biologically plausible lagged effects to assess temporal dynamics.

This step separates ecological signal from observation noise.









Note5. Ecological Validation





Predictions are only meaningful if validated against nature:

Use independent field observations or ethical experiments to check model outputs.

Compare patterns with known natural history to ensure plausibility.

Perform internal validation through cross-validation, simulation, and residual diagnostics.

Validation ensures that statistical associations reflect true ecological processes.









Note6. Deliver & Iterate





Finally, I communicate and refine findings to produce a deliverable (and then reiterate!):

Interpret model results in ecological terms (e.g., probability change in bird song following a monkey call).

Report uncertainty with credible or prediction intervals.

Iterate the model and study design based on validation feedback, refining features, lag windows, or model structure.

This iterative approach produces robust, biologically meaningful insights that can guide conservation, management, or further experimentation.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
Example Application
Suppose we want to know whether monkey howls suppress bird song. Using the NESTED framework:

Name the Alternative Hypotheses: Identify plausible explanations and competing mechanisms, such as direct suppression, temporal avoidance, or coincidental activity overlap.
Embed Natural History: Set recorder spacing and bin sizes according to species’ movement behavior; ensure that recorder setting are appropriate for vocal behavior.
Structured Data Collection and Processing: Extract call counts and spectral features; create time-lagged predictors.
Test with Models: Fit hierarchical time-series models with detection uncertainty.
Ecological Validation: Compare predictions with field playback experiments and observations.
Deliver & Iterate: Report effect sizes (probability of bird song change per time lag) and refine with new data; devise new and improved study approaches.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
Conclusion
The NESTED framework combines statistical rigor with deep ecological understanding, providing a repeatable approach for behavioral-ecology research. By explicitly naming alternative hypotheses and embedding natural history at every step, models are not only statistically sound but also ecologically meaningful, leading to actionable insights about species interactions in the wild.

title: Committee service request
section: A short reading list (some of what I would recommend in my capacity as a committee member)
text:
A short reading list (some of what I would recommend in my capacity as a committee member)
Below are some of the papers that I think every graduate student in ecology (especially behavioral ecology) should read. These are some examples of papers I would recommend for your oral/written exams. Some are effectively zombie ideas, but they help provide some historical context. These are in no particular order (except the first paper, which is what everyone should read):

Franks et al. (2025). Ecology needs a causal overhaul. Biological Reviews.
Ecology must adopt explicit causal inference frameworks beyond statistical associations.
Sih et al. (2004). Behavioral syndromes: an ecological and evolutionary overview. Trends in Ecology & Evolution.
Correlated behaviors constrain plasticity and shape ecological, evolutionary outcomes.
Wong and Candolin. (2015). Behavioral responses to changing environments. Behavioral Ecology.
Behavioral plasticity mediates species persistence under rapid anthropogenic environmental change.
Chave. (2013). The problem of pattern and scale in ecology: what have we learned in 20 years? Ecology Letters.
Ecological processes vary across scales; linking pattern and process remains central.
Duckworth. (2009). The role of behavior in evolution: a search for mechanism. Evolutionary Ecology.
Behavior can drive or inhibit evolution depending on mechanisms and context.
Murtaugh. (2007). Simplicity and complexity in ecological data analysis. Ecology.
Simpler analyses often outperform complex models for clarity and reproducibility.
Hobbs and Hilborn. (2006). Alternatives to statistical hypothesis testing in ecology. Ecological Applications.
Likelihood and Bayesian methods enable richer inference than traditional hypothesis testing.
Hurlbert. (1984). Pseudoreplication and the design of ecological field experiments. Ecological Monographs.
Improper replication undermines inference; experimental design must match hypotheses.
Laland et al. (2016). An introduction to niche construction theory. Evolutionary Ecology.
Organisms modify environments, feeding back to influence evolutionary processes.
Levin et al. (1992). The problem of pattern and scale in ecology. Ecology.
Ecological patterns emerge from scale-dependent processes requiring cross-scale integration.
McEwen et al. (2003). The concept of allostasis in biology and biomedicine. Hormones and Behavior.
Organisms maintain stability through change, balancing energy demands and stressors.
Odling-Smee et al. (2013). Niche construction theory: a practical guide for ecologists. Quarterly Review of Biology.
Environmental modification by organisms generates eco-evolutionary feedbacks and inheritance.
Schank et al. (2009). Pseudoreplication is a pseudoproblem. Journal of Comparative Psychology.
Critiques pseudoreplication doctrine, emphasizing context-dependent statistical inference and design.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
In behavioral ecology, understanding interactions between species requires more than just statistical analysis. Passive acoustic recordings, camera traps, or observational data provide exceedingly rich datasets, but they are often noisy, incomplete, and influenced by complex ecological, behavioral, and observation processes. Application of generalized linear (or additive) models –even using mixed-effect designs– very commonly break the underlying pattern-generating processes that we study.
In this short guide, I describe the NESTED framework, which offers a logical, repeatable pipeline for tackling questions that integrate statistical modeling with ecological validation, always grounded in natural history knowledge. NESTED has the following steps:

Name the Alternative Hypotheses
Embed Natural History
Structured Data Collection and Processing
Test with Models
Ecological Validation
Deliver & Iterate

A central addition to this framework is an explicit structural causal modeling (SCM) / directed acyclic graph (DAG) approach. The DAG does not replace natural history; it formalizes our empirical understanding. It helps translate ecological understanding and reasoning into explicit assumptions about causal (i.e. generative) pathways, confounding, mediation, detection, measurement, and time-lagged responses. This is far from being anything novel. But this approach has the following advantages

keep our thoughts in order, allowing us to match statistics with our focal questions
formally identify what variables should and should not be adjusted for
reduces harmful decision-making (by eliminating information theoretic variable selection)
reduces the sample size to information ratio (especially compared to approaches like AIC multi-model selection or Structual Equation Modeling)

Let us go through







Note1. Name the Alternative Hypotheses





The first step is to explicitly define the competing hypotheses that might explain the patterns we observe:

What are the plausible ways one species could affect another?
Could observed patterns result from confounding factors such as habitat, season, time of day, weather, or detectability?
Which hypotheses are consistent with known natural-history traits such as activity patterns, response times, social behavior, movement, and sensory ecology?
Which hypotheses imply a causal effect, and which imply only association, prediction, or description?

At this stage, the goal is to name the alternative explanations before fitting any model. For an SCM/DAG workflow, these hypotheses become candidate causal structures. Each alternative should imply a different set of pathways, such as:

direct effects,
indirect or mediated effects,
shared causes or confounders,
detection or observation processes,
temporal feedbacks or lagged responses.

By naming the alternatives upfront, we create a clear roadmap for testing and ensure that subsequent modeling is hypothesis-driven, causally explicit, and ecologically informed.









Note2. Embed Natural History





Next, we explicitly embed the natural history and ecology of the study species or group of species into the study design and data processing:

Select temporal and spatial scales that match behavior, such as 10-second bins for vocal interactions. This is where one could disagree with Levin (1992).
Select auxiliary data such as temperature, light, noise, location, habitat, season, or observer conditions that could be relevant to the study questions.
Plan manipulative or observational experiments that are ethical and feasible, accounting for the natural history and ecology of the species.
Identify variables that may act as confounders, mediators, moderators, outcomes, or detection processes.

In the SCM/DAG approach, natural history determines the structure of the graph. It helps decide which arrows are plausible, which arrows are implausible, which variables must be measured, and which variables should not be conditioned on for a particular estimand.
Together, these choices help ensure that the dataset captures the variation that actually matters in nature, rather than simply collecting variables that are convenient to measure.









Note3. Structured Data Collection and Processing





I transform raw observations into features suitable for analysis:

For acoustic data, detect and classify calls to produce timestamped event tables.
Aggregate into time bins, calculating call rates, durations, spectral properties, or other biologically relevant measures.
Create time-lagged features to capture potential delayed responses between species.
Record metadata related to detection and observation conditions, such as recorder placement, background noise, distance, weather, or equipment differences.
Preserve information needed to distinguish biological absence from non-detection.

A well-structured dataset is essential for reproducible and interpretable results. In an SCM/DAG workflow, data processing should follow the causal question rather than precede it. For example, if the DAG distinguishes true vocal activity from detected vocal activity, then the dataset should preserve variables that help model detection rather than treating detections as if they were perfect observations.
This step turns the verbal and graphical causal model into measurable variables, time windows, lag structures, and observation units.









Note4. Test with Models





Statistical models quantify associations and, when assumptions are explicit and defensible, causal relationships while respecting ecological realism:

Begin with exploratory analysis such as descriptive statistics, plots, cross-correlation, and temporal summaries to understand the data.
Use hierarchical or time-series models such as GLMMs, GAMMs, Bayesian state-space models, or dynamic models to account for variation among sites, recorders, individuals, or time periods.
Incorporate observation error where detection is imperfect.
Use the DAG to identify the estimand and the appropriate adjustment strategy.
Avoid conditioning on mediators or colliders unless the estimand specifically requires it.
Examine biologically plausible lagged effects to assess temporal dynamics.

This is where the SCM/DAG approach becomes operational. The DAG clarifies whether the model is estimating a total effect, direct effect, indirect effect, mediated pathway, predictive association, or descriptive pattern. For example:

A total effect model should adjust for confounders but generally avoid adjusting for mediators.
A controlled direct effect model may condition on mediators if the goal is to isolate a direct pathway.
A mediated effect requires modeling the pathway through the mediator, not simply adding all variables to one model.
A detection-corrected effect may require a model that separates the ecological state from the observation process.

This step separates ecological signal from observation noise and separates causal questions from merely associational or predictive analyses.









Note5. Ecological Validation





Predictions and causal estimates are only meaningful if validated against nature:

Use independent field observations or ethical experiments to check model outputs.
Compare patterns with known natural history to ensure plausibility.
Perform internal validation through cross-validation, simulation, posterior predictive checks, sensitivity analysis, and residual diagnostics.
Check whether the estimated effects are consistent with the causal pathways proposed in the DAG.
Revisit the DAG when results are biologically implausible, inconsistent across scales, or sensitive to assumptions.

Validation ensures that statistical associations and causal estimates reflect true ecological processes rather than artifacts of sampling, detection, model structure, or unmeasured confounding.
In an SCM/DAG workflow, validation is not only about model fit. It is also about testing whether the assumed causal structure remains defensible.









Note6. Deliver & Iterate





Finally, I communicate and refine findings to produce a deliverable, and then reiterate:

Interpret model results in ecological terms, such as the probability change in bird song following a monkey call.
Report uncertainty with credible intervals, confidence intervals, or prediction intervals.
State the estimand clearly: descriptive, associational, predictive, total causal effect, direct effect, indirect effect, or mediated pathway.
Report the DAG or causal assumptions alongside the model.
Explain which assumptions are required for the interpretation.
Iterate the model and study design based on validation feedback, refining features, lag windows, measured covariates, detection models, or DAG structure.

This iterative approach produces robust, biologically meaningful insights that can guide conservation, management, or further experimentation.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
Example Application
Suppose we want to know whether monkey howls suppress bird song. Using the NESTED framework:

Name the Alternative Hypotheses: Identify plausible explanations and competing mechanisms, such as direct suppression, temporal avoidance, coincidental activity overlap, shared responses to time of day, habitat effects, or differences in detectability.
Embed Natural History: Set recorder spacing and bin sizes according to species’ movement behavior; ensure that recorder settings are appropriate for vocal behavior; identify likely time lags based on response time and hearing distance.
Structured Data Collection and Processing: Extract call counts and spectral features; create time-lagged predictors; preserve background noise, recorder location, and detection covariates.
Test with Models: Draw a DAG linking monkey howls, bird song, time of day, weather, habitat, background noise, and detection. Then fit hierarchical time-series models or detection-corrected models aligned with the target estimand.
Ecological Validation: Compare predictions with field playback experiments and direct observations; check whether responses occur at biologically plausible lags and distances.
Deliver & Iterate: Report effect sizes, such as the change in probability of bird song following monkey howls at specific time lags. State whether the result is descriptive, predictive, associational, or causal under the DAG assumptions. Refine the study design and DAG with new data.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
section: A Simple SCM/DAG Framing for the Example
text:
A Simple SCM/DAG Framing for the Example
For the monkey-howl example, a simplified causal structure might include:





flowchart LR
  TOD["**Time of day**"]
  HAB["**Habitat**"]
  WEA["**Weather**"]
  MON["**Monkey howls**"]
  BIRD["**True bird song**"]
  NOISE["**Background noise**"]
  DET["**Detected bird song**"]

  TOD --> MON
  TOD --> BIRD
  HAB --> MON
  HAB --> BIRD
  WEA --> MON
  WEA --> BIRD
  MON --> BIRD
  NOISE --> DET
  BIRD --> DET
  MON --> NOISE

  classDef measured fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#5b8db8,stroke-width:3px,color:#000,font-size:17px;
  classDef outcome fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#5a9a5a,stroke-width:3px,color:#000,font-size:17px;
  classDef obs fill:#f4b6b6,stroke:#b35c5c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000,font-size:17px;

  class TOD,HAB,WEA,MON,NOISE measured;
  class BIRD outcome;
  class DET obs;






This DAG makes the target clearer. If the estimand is the total causal effect of monkey howls on true bird song, we would adjust for shared causes such as time of day, habitat, and weather, but we would avoid adjusting for variables downstream of monkey howls if they mediate part of the effect. If the observed response is detected bird song rather than true bird song, then the observation process must also be considered.

title: NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research
text:
Conclusion
The NESTED framework combines statistical rigor with deep ecological understanding, providing a repeatable approach for behavioral-ecology research. By explicitly naming alternative hypotheses, embedding natural history, and formalizing assumptions with SCMs/DAGs, models are not only statistically sound but also aligned with the ecological question being asked.
The key benefit is that the analysis becomes estimand-aware. Instead of simply asking which predictors belong in a model, the NESTED framework asks:

What ecological process are we trying to understand, what causal assumptions are we making, and what quantity are we trying to estimate?

That shift helps behavioral ecologists produce results that are clearer, more defensible, and more useful for understanding species interactions in the wild.

title: BCL Guidebooks
text:
Guidebooks
The work of the Behavioral Complexity Lab and its members is only possible through the support of the following agencies, organizations, and programs. The BCL has brought in >$6.6M in grant funding.


















NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research


Need a quick plan for designing a project?












Writing a short science paper (for those with short attention spans)


Have a short attention span for writing and reading? Here’s a sentence-by-sentence paper recipe!










NESTED: A Structured Framework for Behavioral-Ecology Research








No matching items

title: Join
text:
Join the lab!
The Behavioral Complexity Lab (BCL) typically has 2-3 graduate students and 3-4 undergraduate students at any time. The Lab holds weekly lab meetings with its sister lab (the Tarwater Lab of Tropical Ecology and Behavior). Perks of being a BCL member include, but are not limited to:

inclusion within a relaxed and non-$#%%y research environment
access to all lab equipment (including computing resources and equipment)
free coffee and tea in the lab
personal desk space for your lab research (and to use for studying between classes)
opportunity to develop an independent or guided research project (few cookie-cutter projects)
opportunity to contribute and potentially co-author lab projects
gaining experience with grant writing (big and small)
encouragement of work-life balance (though this might mean working on weekends during a field season!)
lots of “small” datasets for you to play with and help get over the finish line!





Current opportunities for new Ph.D. and M.Sc. students

From Patrick Kelley– I am not currently seeking Ph.D. or M.Sc. students (mainly because two really awesome ones recently graduated, and my sole remaining one is doing some amazing work!). My desire to not accept for students right now is not for lack of interest but rather a lack of financial support. The one thing that keeps me up at night is the possibility of not being able to pay folks in my lab. So, I like to be very sure that anyone in my lab never has to experience the stress of not getting a paycheck. That said, if you have your own funding, I am more than happy to speak with you about opportunities. And I may even be able to provide a small bit of supplementary support for housing, field station fees, and equipment. Feel free to send me an email introducing yourself; at the very least, this is a good chance to meet someone new and interesting (you, not me). Please feel free to send the following to me via email:

A brief statement of your research interests and your previous experience. (Please ensure that this statement is relevant to what my lab does. Unfortunately, it’s necessary to ignore a large number of emails from folks who clearly have not read what I do and, more important, what I do not do.)
A brief statement on how you think ecologists should identify causes in ecological systems. For example, when is an experiment necessary, and when is it not? Have fun with this one!
Your recent curriculum vitae (CV). (I do not need to read the usual business-like verbiage often included in a typical resume, the windbag cousin of the CV.)
A picture of a critter (or, better yet, an interaction between critters) at your favorite field site! (When I’m in the office, it is always fun to live vicariously through others!)







Current opportunities for Graduate Student Research Assistants

From Patrick Kelley– If you are a current graduate researcher (at University of Wyoming or another institution) who wants to gain new experience with projects in our lab, contact me. I welcomed two graduate students from the UW Department of Botany into my lab to work on programming projects; as Graduate Computing Scholars funded by UW’s School of Computing they each received tuition, fees, and stipend for the academic year, and (I think) we had wonderful experiences working on new problems together! To inquire about such a possibility, send me an email with the following details:

A brief statement outlining your thesis/dissertation research
A brief statement outlining the specific reasons why you want to hang out in my lab and work with me







Current opportunities for Undergraduate students

From Patrick Kelley– If you’re an undergraduate looking to get research experience, our lab offers lots of ways to get involved! Students who start with lab-based projects (video annotation, bioacoustics, etc.) may later join fieldwork opportunities in places like Panama or Hawaii. To learn more, send me an email with the following details:

Your recent curriculum vitae (CV).
A brief statement outlining the research topics that interest you and your reasons for choosing them.
A brief statement outlining the specific reasons why you want to join my lab (in your chosen capacity).

title: Selected Ecology Papers Bibliography
text:
Sih et al. (2004). Behavioral syndromes: an ecological and evolutionary overview. Trends in Ecology & Evolution.
Correlated behaviors constrain plasticity and shape ecological, evolutionary outcomes.
Wong et al. (2015). Behavioral responses to changing environments. Behavioral Ecology.
Behavioral plasticity mediates species persistence under rapid anthropogenic environmental change.
Chave et al. (2013). The problem of pattern and scale in ecology: what have we learned in 20 years? Ecology Letters.
Ecological processes vary across scales; linking pattern and process remains central.
Duckworth et al. (2009). The role of behavior in evolution: a search for mechanism. Evolutionary Ecology.
Behavior can drive or inhibit evolution depending on mechanisms and context.
Murtaugh et al. (2007). Simplicity and complexity in ecological data analysis. Ecology.
Simpler analyses often outperform complex models for clarity and reproducibility.
Franks et al. (2025). Ecology needs a causal overhaul. Biological Reviews.
Ecology must adopt explicit causal inference frameworks beyond statistical associations.
Hobbs et al. (2006). Alternatives to statistical hypothesis testing in ecology. Ecological Applications.
Likelihood and Bayesian methods enable richer inference than traditional hypothesis testing.
Hurlbert et al. (1984). Pseudoreplication and the design of ecological field experiments. Ecological Monographs.
Improper replication undermines inference; experimental design must match hypotheses.
Laland et al. (2016). An introduction to niche construction theory. Evolutionary Ecology.
Organisms modify environments, feeding back to influence evolutionary processes.
Levin et al. (1992). The problem of pattern and scale in ecology. Ecology.
Ecological patterns emerge from scale-dependent processes requiring cross-scale integration.
McEwen et al. (2003). The concept of allostasis in biology and biomedicine. Hormones and Behavior.
Organisms maintain stability through change, balancing energy demands and stressors.
Odling-Smee et al. (2013). Niche construction theory: a practical guide for ecologists. Quarterly Review of Biology.
Environmental modification by organisms generates eco-evolutionary feedbacks and inheritance.
Schank et al. (2009). Pseudoreplication is a pseudoproblem. Journal of Comparative Psychology.
Critiques pseudoreplication doctrine, emphasizing context-dependent statistical inference and design.

Hawaii VINE Project


​


Using multiple sites across the island of Oahu, the Hawaii VINE (Vertebrate Introductions and Novel Ecosystems) Project examines [1] how the structure and dynamics of seed dispersal networks vary across ecological contexts, [2] how seed dispersal competence of non-native species varies across ecological contexts to influence ecosystem functioning, and [3] how ecosystem functioning is maintained across new spatial and temporal extents.


​


The Hawaiian Islands are both the extinction and invasive species capitals of the world. The result has been Hawaiian ecosystems fundamentally changed in form; that is, ecosystems replete with a mix of novel and native species. Most native Hawaiian plant species are bird-dispersed, yet no native avian dispersers remain in most Hawaiian ecosystems. Thus, ecosystem functioning will only be maintained by the handful of invasive vertebrate dispersers that now reside on the islands, most of which are birds. The Hawaii VINE Project seeks to determine how well different species of non-native birds disperse native plant species across environments, whether non-native rat species have a cumulative positive (via seed dispersal) or negative (via predation on bird seed-dispersers and/or via seed predation) impact on plant communities, and creating predictive models to be used to improve non-native plant management plans, while facilitating the recovery of native threatened, endangered, and at-risk plants.


 


The Hawaii VINE Project relies on extensive field-based data collection (collected year-round for four years), including diet sampling, behavioral observations of birds and rats, small mammal and bird censusing, and field experiments. We apply ecological modeling techniques such as hierarchical occupancy modeling and movement ecology models to describe and quantify seed dispersal effectiveness in novel Hawaiian communities.











Please visit and like the Hawaii VINE Project Facebook page by clicking on the graphic at right. There, you will receive notifications about field work and publications.
